text: Exercise your dog beforehand. A satisfied dog rests easier than a restless one.
Skip the long goodbye. Cheerful departures teach your dog that leaving isn't a big deal.
Leave a high-value chew or stuffed Kong he only gets during rehearsals.
Always use the same departure cue word, like "I'll be back."
Step outside fully — closed door, not a peek-out.
Resist the urge to check the camera every 30 seconds. Trust the timer.
If he barks early, wait it out. Coming back during barking can teach him barking works.
If he barks the whole time, end early and shorten next session. He's telling you the jump was too big.
Don't return mid-bark. Wait for a 5–10 second pause, even if it means waiting longer than planned.
Walk away from the door, ideally out of earshot. Lingering can give away your presence and undermine the rehearsal.
Use a dog camera to monitor from a distance. It lets you watch behavior and hear barking without needing to stay within earshot of the door.
Enter calmly. No big greeting and no high-pitched voice.
Let your pet settle on their own before petting or treats.
Avoid making a celebration of your return. Boring entries help reduce anticipation.
Pacing, panting, and whining count as stress signals, not just barking.
A dog who eats during your absence is a good sign. A dog who won't touch food is over threshold.
Destruction near doors or windows means panic, not boredom. Shorten the next session significantly.
Drooling or accidents during a rehearsal are major red flags. Step way back.
Practice when you're not actually leaving. "Pretend departures" prevent the cue from only meaning "alone time."
Vary the rehearsal length. Predictable patterns make dogs anxious about the long ones.
Mix in short rehearsals even after long successes. Unpredictability builds confidence.
Practice at different times of day. Evening departures can feel different than daytime.
One bad session isn't a setback. Two or three in a row means revisit your last successful length.
Don't push through a fail. Repeating the same length until it's a Great is the fastest path forward.
Big life changes (new home, illness, schedule shift) can reset progress. Be patient.
Holidays, fireworks, or storms can affect a sensitive dog for days. Pause and resume gently.
Progress isn't linear. The trainer's chart looked like stairs, not a ramp, for a reason.
Compare your dog to himself last month, not to other dogs.
Rehearsals build a skill. Skills take repetition, not just time.
Celebrate the boring rehearsals — boring means he's confident.
